articles.title: Decolonizing the Curriculum, Episode 2: The Seminoles and land grant universities

narrative:
Short impact summary

Published by Peter Lang Verlag in 2019, James V. Grant's The Curriculum of Horror occupies a distinctive niche at the intersection of curriculum theory, art, and psychology. The book has established a presence in commercial retail channels and appears in a small number of library collections, while thematic affinities with open courseware materials suggest modest but genuine pedagogical reach. Formal citation uptake remains limited to date, placing the work in the early stages of scholarly diffusion.

Full narrative

Grant's monograph draws on curriculum studies, sociology, and psychology to interrogate the role that horror—as artistic genre and affective experience—plays in educational contexts. The conceptual architecture of the book spans pedagogy, art, and the psychological dimensions of fear and discomfort, positioning horror not as peripheral spectacle but as a legitimate and underexplored lens through which curricula can be examined. This interdisciplinary framing situates the work within broader conversations about what counts as legitimate knowledge in formal and informal learning environments.

The book's most robust signal of reach comes from its commercial presence: it is listed on major retail and reader platforms, indicating that it has found an audience beyond specialist academic library circuits. Physical holdings, while currently limited to a small number of institutions, confirm that the work has entered at least some library collections. Nine appearances across open courseware environments—including materials from MIT OpenCourseWare and Open University OpenLearn—suggest that adjacent instructors working on genre, narrative, and educational storytelling have encountered thematic terrain that resonates with Grant's project, even where direct citation is not recorded.

The book carries a meaningful alignment with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goal of Quality Education, reflecting its normative interest in expanding and enriching what and how educators teach. This policy-adjacent dimension, while not the book's primary register, positions it as a contribution to ongoing debates about inclusive and critically engaged curriculum design. The work's percentile ranking within its publication cohort is currently modest, consistent with the early-stage diffusion typical of specialized scholarly monographs in the humanities and social sciences.

Overall, The Curriculum of Horror represents a targeted scholarly intervention in curriculum theory that has secured commercial visibility and some pedagogical adjacency, while its formal citation record remains at an early stage. For a 2019 monograph operating at the margins of curriculum studies and cultural theory, these patterns are not unusual; the book's longer-term influence will depend on whether scholars in education, horror studies, and critical pedagogy engage with its arguments as the field continues to expand.
